title: Galois Representations Associated to Holomorphic Limits of Discrete Series
speaker: Wushi Goldring
abstract: We attach Galois representations to automorphic representations on unitary groups whose weight (=component at infinity) is a holomorphic limit of discrete series. The main innovation is a new construction of congruences, using the Hasse Invariant, which avoids q-expansions and so is applicable in much greater generality than previous methods. Our result is a natural generalization of the classical Deligne-Serre Theorem on weight one modular forms and work of Taylor on GSp(4).

proof_stages.label: Motivating landscape of archimedean types
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that moving from discrete series to holomorphic, non-holomorphic, and degenerate limits worsens the available cohomological and algebraic-geometric tools, while natural motives and known cases point to holomorphic limits for unitary groups as the next target.

proof_stages.label: Root-theoretic meaning of limits of discrete series
proof_stages.summary: After fixing a connected simple real group with compact Cartan, the talk defines Harish-Chandra parameters, recalls the parameterization of discrete series by regular parameters, and characterizes singular parameters giving nondegenerate and holomorphic limits of discrete series.

proof_stages.label: Main theorem for unitary groups
proof_stages.summary: For a unitary group attached to CM data and a Shimura datum, the speaker states that a cuspidal automorphic representation whose archimedean component is a holomorphic limit of discrete series has a unique l-adic Galois representation, under finite-place, splitting, and unramifiedness hypotheses, compatible with local Langlands at suitable split unramified primes.

proof_stages.label: Deligne–Serre–Taylor congruence strategy
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is modeled on multiplying a weight-one modular form by powers of an Eisenstein series congruent to 1 modulo powers of p, thereby moving to cohomological weights and using Taylor’s pseudorepresentations to pass from congruent Hecke data to Galois representations.

proof_stages.label: Hasse invariant replacement for q-expansions
proof_stages.summary: To avoid q-expansions for unitary Shimura varieties, the argument replaces the Eisenstein-series congruence by a geometrically defined Hasse invariant, constructed for abelian schemes in characteristic l via Frobenius, Verschiebung, and the determinant of the Hodge bundle.

proof_stages.label: Geometric properties of the Hasse invariant
proof_stages.summary: The Hasse invariant is shown to be compatible with isogenies, to define a well-behaved mod-l automorphic form on the Shimura variety, to have a characteristic-zero lift after taking a power, and to be nonzero precisely under the ordinary-points condition supplied by the splitting of l in the reflex field.

proof_stages.label: Hecke congruences and extraction of the Galois representation
proof_stages.summary: Using integral automorphic forms in place of q-expansions, the speaker states the key congruence comparing Hecke action on powers of the lifted Hasse invariant times a holomorphic-limit form with the Hecke action on the original form, and then applies Taylor’s pseudorepresentation method to obtain the theorem.

examples.name: Tate modules of abelian varieties
examples.note: Abelian varieties of dimensions 1, 2, and at least 3 are used to illustrate how natural motivic Galois representations lead respectively to discrete series, holomorphic limits, and degenerate limits.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites Tate modules of abelian varieties as desired Galois representations and states how the associated infinity type varies with dimension, without computing or constructing a Tate module

examples.name: Eisenstein series mod l as the elliptic Hasse invariant
examples.note: The observation that the reduction of the Eisenstein series E_{l-1} is the Hasse invariant of the universal elliptic curve is presented as the source of the replacement of q-expansion congruences by a general Hasse-invariant construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the Eisenstein series congruence concretely, multiplying a weight-one form by it, tracking the resulting congruence and weight, and relating its mod-l reduction to the Hasse invariant

passes.reason: The mod-l Eisenstein series/Hasse invariant is a standard modular-form construction tool rather than a sporadic, extremal, or adversarial example.
The mod-l Eisenstein series/Hasse invariant is used positively as the standard replacement for q-expansion congruences.
The mod-l reduction of the normalized Eisenstein series E_{l-1} has collapsed q-expansion 1 as the elliptic Hasse invariant, used constructively for congruences.
